#T_SP=custom.Span|label
#Text=LA CUISINE CREOLE\r
#Text=\r
#Text=                                SOUP\r
#Text=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Soup being the first course served at all ordinary dinners, we make\r
#Text=it the basis for preliminary remarks.
#Text=Nothing more palatable than\r
#Text=good, well-made soup, and nothing less appetising than poor soup.
#Text=Now\r
#Text=to attain perfection in any line, care and attention are requisite,\r
#Text=careful study a necessity, and application the moving force.
#Text=Hence,\r
#Text=cooking in all its branches should be studied as a science, and not\r
#Text=be looked upon as a haphazard mode of getting through life.
#Text=Cooking\r
#Text=is in a great measure a chemical process, and the ingredients of\r
#Text=certain dishes should be as carefully weighed and tested as though\r
#Text=emanating from the laboratory.
#Text=Few female cooks think of this, but\r
#Text=men with their superior instinctive reasoning power are more governed\r
#Text=by law and abide more closely to rule; therefore, are better cooks,\r
#Text=and command higher prices for services.
#Text=Now, with regard to soup making, the first care is to have the fire\r
#Text=brisk, the vessel in which it is cooked thoroughly cleaned and free\r
#Text=from odor.
#Text=To insure this, keep one vessel sacred to soup as nearly\r
#Text=as possible; and after serving wash the pot with potash water, or\r
#Text=take a piece of washing soda the size of a nutmeg, dissolve in hot\r
#Text=water and then cleanse the vessel.
#Text=A good workman is known by his\r
#Text=tools, so also a good cook will look well to the utensils before\r
#Text=commencing operations.
#Text=Good results follow carefulness.
#Text=_Soup must have time_ to cook, and should always boil gently, that\r
#Text=the meat may become tender, and give out its juices.
#Text=Allow a quart\r
#Text=of water and a teaspoonful of salt for each pound of meat.
#Text=Soup meat\r
#Text=must always be put down in cold water.
#Text=Skim well before it comes\r
#Text=to the boiling point, and again skim off superfluous fat before\r
#Text=putting in the vegetables.
#Text=The vegetables most used in soups are\r
#Text=carrots, leeks, parsley, turnip, celery, tomatoes, okras, cabbage,\r
#Text=cauliflower, peas and potatoes.
#Text=One large leek, two carrots, one bunch of parsley, two turnips and a\r
#Text=potato, will be enough for one pot of soup.
#Text=One head of celery, two\r
#Text=leeks, two turnips, and five or six small potatoes will be enough\r
#Text=another time.
#Text=Six tomatoes skinned, the juice strained from the\r
#Text=seeds, a leek, a bunch of parsley, and six potatoes will answer for\r
#Text=another style; a carrot, some cabbage, tomatoes, and potatoes will\r
#Text=do another time.
#Text=Okra alone is vegetable enough for a gombo, unless\r
#Text=onion is liked with it.
#Text=Green peas, lettuce, and new potatoes are\r
#Text=enough for spring lamb soup.
#Text=Vermicelli and macaroni are for chicken,\r
#Text=lamb or veal soup, with the addition of onion if liked.
#Text=It is well to prepare the vegetables when the meat is put over the\r
#Text=fire to boil; allow a quart of water to a pound of meat.
#Text=Trim and\r
#Text=scrape carrots, then cut or grate them.
#Text=Wash parsley and cut it\r
#Text=small.
#Text=Pare turnips and cut them in slices a quarter of an inch\r
#Text=thick.
#Text=Cut leeks in thick slices.
#Text=Cut celery in half lengths; the\r
#Text=delicate green leaves give a fine flavor to the soup.
#Text=Pour boiling water on tomatoes, which will cause the skins to peel\r
#Text=off easily; when cool, squeeze out the seeds, and reserve the juice\r
#Text=for use in soup.
#Text=Shave cabbage in thin slices.
#Text=Slice okra for gombo or okra soup.
#Text=Pare\r
#Text=the potatoes, shell the peas, and cut off green corn from the cob,\r
#Text=for all these add fine flavor to soup.
#Text=To color soup brown, use browned flour or a little burnt sugar.
#Text=Spinach leaves give a fine green color.
#Text=Pound the leaves, tie them\r
#Text=in a cloth, and squeeze out all the juice which add to the soup\r
#Text=five minutes before serving.
#Text=This is also used to give color to\r
#Text=mock-turtle soup.
#Text=You may color soup red by putting in the strained juice of tomatoes,\r
#Text=or the whole tomato, if it is run through a sieve; grated carrot\r
#Text=gives a fine amber color; okra gives a pale green.
#Text=For white soups, which are made of veal, lamb, and chicken, white\r
#Text=vegetables are best, such as rice, pearl barley, vermicelli, and\r
#Text=macaroni; the thickening should then be made of unbrowned flour.
#Text=STOCK FOR SOUP\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Stock in its composition is not confined to any set rules for any\r
#Text=particular proportions.
#Text=All cook books give particular as well as\r
#Text=general directions for its manufacture; but all cooks know that\r
#Text=the most economical plan is to have a general stock-pot, where,\r
#Text=or into which, you can throw any pieces of beef or any piece of\r
#Text=meat from which gravy can be extracted--bones, skin, brisket or\r
#Text=tops of ribs, ox-cheek, ham, trimmings of turkey and other fowls,\r
#Text=pieces of mutton, bacon, veal, game, etc., etc.
#Text=In fact, anything\r
#Text=that will become a jelly will assist in making stock.
#Text=To this\r
#Text=medley of ingredients add pepper, salt, spices, herbs, carrots cut\r
#Text=small, onions, and curry, if wished, etc., and stew all to a rich\r
#Text=consistency over a slow fire, and then remove to cool.
#Text=When cool, or\r
#Text=rather cold, every particle of fat must be removed and stock poured\r
#Text=clear of all sediment; it is now ready for use.
#Text=When very rich soup\r
#Text=is desired, the jelly from a cow-heel, or lump of butter rolled in\r
#Text=flour, must be added to the stock.
#Text=TO CLARIFY STOCKS OR SOUPS\r
#Text=\r
#Text=The whites of two eggs to about four quarts of stock or soup; two\r
#Text=pints and a half of cold water.
#Text=Whisk the whites of two fresh eggs with half a pint of water for ten\r
#Text=minutes; then pour in very gently the four quarts of boiling stock or\r
#Text=soup, stirring it all the time.
#Text=Place the stewpan over the fire, and\r
#Text=skim the mixture till clear before allowing it to boil.
#Text=When on the\r
#Text=point of boiling, stir rapidly; then place it a little back from the\r
#Text=fire, and let it settle till the whites of the eggs become separated.
#Text=Strain it through a fine cloth placed over a sieve, and it will be\r
#Text=clear and good.
#Text=STOCK FOR GRAVIES\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Cut the meat from a knuckle of veal, and put it, with a pound of\r
#Text=lean beef, into two quarts of water; add one table-spoonful of salt\r
#Text=and a teaspoonful of pepper; cover it close, and let it stew until\r
#Text=the meat is very tender; then strain it and keep it for rich soups\r
#Text=or gravies, as thinning them with water spoils them.
#Text=Always keep a\r
#Text=pot or stewpan in which to throw all nice pieces of meat left from\r
#Text=dinner, also any steak, bones, chicken wings, etc., etc.
#Text=This makes\r
#Text=a reserve of stock with very little fresh meat.
#Text=It is useful and\r
#Text=economical, and, being without vegetables, never sours.
#Text=In making\r
#Text=oyster soup use a pint or so of this stock to the usual quart of\r
#Text=oysters and a pint of milk.
#Text=PLAIN BEEF SOUP\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Five pounds of the leg or shin of beef; one gallon of water; a\r
#Text=teaspoonful of salt; two heads of celery; five carrots; three onions;\r
#Text=four turnips; two tomatoes, and a bunch of sweet herbs.
#Text=Boil four\r
#Text=hours and a half.
#Text=Cut the meat in two or three pieces, and put them into a pot with a\r
#Text=gallon of cold water, which gradually soaks out the juices of the\r
#Text=meat before coming to the boil.
#Text=Salt well, then skim as the soup\r
#Text=heats.
#Text=Boil slowly with a regular heat for about four hours; then\r
#Text=add two heads of celery, five carrots cut small, two tomatoes, three\r
#Text=onions sliced and fried, and the sweet herbs tied up in muslin.
#Text=The\r
#Text=turnips should be added half an hour before serving.
#Text=If any portion\r
#Text=of the meat is required for the table, take it from the soup about\r
#Text=two hours before dinner.
#Text=Let the remainder be left in the soup, which\r
#Text=must be strained through a hair sieve before it is served.
#Text=CRAYFISH BISQUE.
#Text=A CREOLE DISH\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Parboil the fish, pick out the meat, and mince or pound it in a\r
#Text=mortar until very fine; it will require about fifty crayfish.
#Text=Add\r
#Text=to the fish one-third the quantity of bread soaked in milk, and a\r
#Text=quarter of a pound of butter, also salt to taste, a bunch of thyme,\r
#Text=two leaves of sage, a small piece of garlic and a chopped onion.
#Text=Mix\r
#Text=all well and cook ten minutes, stirring all the time to keep it from\r
#Text=growing hard.
#Text=Clean the heads of the fish, throw them in strong salt\r
#Text=and water for a few minutes and then drain them.
#Text=Fill each one with\r
#Text=the above stuffing, flour them, and fry a light brown.
#Text=Set a clean\r
#Text=stewpan over a slow fire, put into it three spoonfuls of lard or\r
#Text=butter, a slice of ham or bacon, two onions chopped fine; dredge over\r
#Text=it enough flour to absorb the grease, then add a pint and a half of\r
#Text=boiling water, or better still, plain beef stock.
#Text=Season this with a\r
#Text=bunch of thyme, a bay leaf, and salt and pepper to taste.
#Text=Let it cook\r
#Text=slowly for half an hour, then put the heads of the crayfish in and\r
#Text=let them boil fifteen minutes.
#Text=Serve rice with it.
#Text=TO DRESS A TURTLE\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Cut off the head and let it bleed well.
#Text=Separate the bottom shell\r
#Text=from the top with care, for fear of breaking the gall bag.
#Text=Throw\r
#Text=the liver and eggs, if any, into a bowl of water.
#Text=Slice off all the\r
#Text=meat from the under-shell and put in water also; break the shell in\r
#Text=pieces, wash carefully and place it in a pot; cover it with water,\r
#Text=and add one pound of middling or flitch of bacon with four chopped\r
#Text=onions.
#Text=Set this on the fire to boil.
#Text=(If preferred, open and clean\r
#Text=the chitterlings or intestines also--some use them.)
#Text=Let this boil\r
#Text=gently for four hours; keep the liver to fry.
#Text=While the under-shell\r
#Text=is boiling, wash the top-shell neatly, cut all the meat out, cover it\r
#Text=up and set it by.
#Text=Parboil the fins, clean them perfectly; take off\r
#Text=the black skin and throw them into water.
#Text=Now cut the flesh removed\r
#Text=from both shells into small pieces; cut the fins up; sprinkle with\r
#Text=salt, cover and set them by.
#Text=When the pot containing the shells,\r
#Text=etc., has boiled four hours, take out the bacon, scrape the shell,\r
#Text=clean and strain the liquor, pour back in the pot about one quart,\r
#Text=and put the rest by for the soup (Turtle Soup No. 2).
#Text=Pick out the\r
#Text=nice pieces strained out, and put with the fins in the gravy.
#Text=Add\r
#Text=to the meat one bottle of wine, one gill mushroom catsup, one gill\r
#Text=of lemon pickle, cloves, nutmeg, salt, pepper, and one pound fresh\r
#Text=butter rolled in flour.
#Text=Stew together; take out the herbs, thicken\r
#Text=with flour and put in the shell to bake with a puff paste around it.
#Text=Trim with eggs.
#Text=“GRENOUILLES FRITES,” OR FRIED FROGS\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Use only the hind-quarters of the frogs.
#Text=After washing them in warm\r
#Text=water, soak well; then put them into cold vinegar with a little\r
#Text=salt, and let them remain one or two hours, after which throw them\r
#Text=into scalding water, and remove the skin without tearing the flesh.
#Text=Wipe them dry, dust flour on them and fry in butter or sweet oil,\r
#Text=with plenty of chopped parsley.
#Text=When brown, dust pepper and a little\r
#Text=salt over them, and garnish with crisped parsley.
#Text=Stewed frogs are\r
#Text=seasoned with butter, wine, beaten eggs and parsley chopped fine.
#Text=COLD MEATS AND HOW TO SERVE THEM\r
#Text=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=                      TO SERVE PICKLED OYSTERS\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Take them from the pickle jar, put them into a glass dish, and\r
#Text=ornament it with the tender, delicate leaves of celery and parsley.
#Text=Serve with bread and butter sandwiches.
#Text=TO SERVE MEAT OR CHICKEN PIE\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Lay a fringed napkin in a waiter or plate larger than the dish in\r
#Text=which the pie is baked; set the pie on it; turn up the edges of the\r
#Text=napkin against it, and put sprigs of parsley or delicate green leaves\r
#Text=of celery on the edge of the plate to keep the napkin in place.
#Text=A NICE WAY TO SERVE COLD MEAT\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Cut cold roast beef in slices, put gravy enough to cover them, add\r
#Text=two tablespoonfuls of wine or catsup.
#Text=If there is not enough gravy,\r
#Text=make more by putting hot water and a good bit of butter, with a\r
#Text=spoonful of browned flour.
#Text=Let it stew gently.
#Text=If liked, a sliced\r
#Text=leek with a bunch of parsley may be added.
#Text=Serve mashed potatoes with\r
#Text=it.
#Text=This is equal to beef a la mode.
#Text=CHICKEN CURRY\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Cut up the chicken and stew as usual for the table.
#Text=When done add a\r
#Text=tablespoonful of curry powder.
#Text=Serve rice with the dish.
#Text=CHICKEN PIE, A LA REINE\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Cut two chickens up as for frying, lay some veal cut in small pieces\r
#Text=in the bottom of your pie dish, cut up over the veal a slice of fat\r
#Text=ham; on this place your chickens; place hard-boiled yolks of eggs in\r
#Text=among the chicken.
#Text=Take half a pint of white sauce, made with butter,\r
#Text=flour, and milk or water; pour this over the chickens, season with a\r
#Text=cup of chopped mushrooms, some parsley, pepper and salt (a good pie\r
#Text=can be made if you omit the mushrooms and ham, but not so rich as\r
#Text=this recipe); now cover your pie with a good paste, and bake for an\r
#Text=hour or two.
#Text=TO COOK SPINACH\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Wash in two or three waters, as the grit adheres very closely to\r
#Text=spinach; when well washed, boil it one half hour in clear water; add\r
#Text=a little soda, if it does not look a nice green.
#Text=When soft, drain it\r
#Text=well and chop very fine--it cannot be too fine; add butter, salt if\r
#Text=needed, and pepper to taste; garnish with hard-boiled eggs cut in\r
#Text=fancy shapes; or, in early spring, it is nice to poach two or three\r
#Text=eggs, and lay on the freshly cooked spinach.
#Text=ASPARAGUS ON TOAST\r
#Text=\r
#Text=The fresher this vegetable is the better; and in picking and washing\r
#Text=it, all stalks not crisp and tender should be thrown aside.
#Text=Cut off\r
#Text=nearly all the horny white parts, tie the rest in neat bunches, and\r
#Text=boil in salted water for twenty minutes or half an hour; then take it\r
#Text=out, let it drain a minute and lay on buttered toast, the heads all\r
#Text=one way; cover with rich drawn butter sauce.
#Text=INDIAN BREAKFAST CAKES\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Take a quart of milk scalding hot; stir into it as much corn-meal as\r
#Text=will make a thick batter, add of salt and saleratus in fine powder,\r
#Text=each a teaspoonful, and when a little cooled, two well-beaten eggs;\r
#Text=bake in buttered pans, in a quick oven.
#Text=This is a nice breakfast cake.
#Text=DELICATE CAKE OF CORN STARCH\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Take half a pound of sugar, one-fourth of a pound of butter, the\r
#Text=whites of eight eggs, and a quarter of a pound of corn starch mixed\r
#Text=with quarter of a pound of common flour.
#Text=Beat all very light, add to\r
#Text=the flour a teaspoonful of cream of tartar, and a half-spoon of fine\r
#Text=soda (not saleratus).
#Text=Flavor with lemon or rose.
#Text=EASY CAKE FOR YOUNG COOKS\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Take two cups of flour, sift it and to each cup put a teaspoonful of\r
#Text=yeast powder.
#Text=Beat the yolks of three eggs and one cup of fine white\r
#Text=sugar, together with half a cup of water mixed with extract or wine;\r
#Text=beat this well in the yolks and sugar (only half a cup); froth up the\r
#Text=whites of the eggs, add them, and last of all, beat in the flour with\r
#Text=the powder in it.
#Text=Bake quickly in square or jelly cake pans.
#Text=YOUNG COOKS’ JELLY ROLL\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Make the sponge for your jelly roll by taking a cup of white sugar,\r
#Text=one cup of flour, and three eggs.
#Text=Mix, etc.; add baking powder with\r
#Text=the flour.
#Text=Bake in a stewpan with a quick fire; turn the cake out on\r
#Text=a towel when done; spread the jelly while it is still warm and soft,\r
#Text=and roll it carefully.
#Text=Cut it in slices when cold; a spoonful of\r
#Text=water beaten with the eggs makes the cake lighter, as it breaks the\r
#Text=tissue of the eggs if it is added to them when beaten up.
#Text=LEMON PIE\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Grate the rind and express the juice of three lemons; rub together a\r
#Text=cup and a half of powdered sugar and three tablespoonfuls of butter;\r
#Text=beat up the yolks of four eggs, and add to the butter and sugar,\r
#Text=lastly the lemon; bake on a rich puff paste without an upper crust.
#Text=While the pie is baking beat up the whites of the four eggs with\r
#Text=powdered loaf sugar, spread it over the top of the pie when done;\r
#Text=then set back in the oven a few moments to brown lightly.
#Text=PRESERVES, SYRUPS AND FRUIT JELLIES\r
#Text=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=                         HINTS ON PRESERVING\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Preserving kettles should be broad and shallow, with a handle on\r
#Text=each side.
#Text=If you wish to preserve in small quantities, use a small\r
#Text=kettle.
#Text=A charcoal furnace is most desirable in warm weather, as you\r
#Text=can put it where you like, and thus avoid the heat of the kitchen.
#Text=Slow, gentle boiling is absolutely necessary in preserving and\r
#Text=pickling.
#Text=Crushed or loaf sugar should be used for preserves, as it is less\r
#Text=liable to ferment during the long hot summer.
#Text=Jelly bags may be made of cotton, linen, or flannel, and can be made\r
#Text=like an old-fashioned reticule, with a string through the top, to\r
#Text=close and suspend it while dripping.
#Text=It is a mistake to think dark fruits, like raspberries, strawberries,\r
#Text=etc., can be preserved equally well with brown sugar, for the color\r
#Text=of this sugar makes the preserves dark, or rather _dingy_, which is\r
#Text=the proper word.
#Text=Glass is best for keeping preserves in, as they may be examined\r
#Text=without opening the jars.
#Text=When first put up they should be corked\r
#Text=tightly, and dipped into coarse melted sealing-wax.
#Text=TO MAKE PRESERVES\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Most fruits are much easier preserved than jellied.
#Text=Weigh the fruit,\r
#Text=and to each pound of fruit the usual rule is a pound of sugar; make a\r
#Text=syrup of the sugar with a half pint of water to each pound of fruit.
#Text=Boil it clear, then put in the fruit and cook it well, and boil\r
#Text=gently till the fruit is clear.
#Text=TO GREEN FRUIT FOR PICKLING OR PRESERVING\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Put vine leaves under, between, and over the fruit in a brass kettle,\r
#Text=and over the leaves sprinkle a teaspoonful of beaten or ground alum;\r
#Text=cover the fruit to be greened, with water, and boil it gently with\r
#Text=the leaves and alum; if not a fine green, take more leaves and dust a\r
#Text=little saleratus over them.
#Text=Spread them out to cool when green, and\r
#Text=proceed to preserve or pickle them as desired.
#Text=TO PRESERVE PEACHES\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Select white clings if you desire to preserve them whole.
#Text=Yellow\r
#Text=peaches make the most transparent preserve, but cannot always be\r
#Text=procured.
#Text=If white clings are convenient, peel and weigh them, and to\r
#Text=each pound of fruit put one pound of sugar and half a pint of water.
#Text=Put the syrup to boil, clarify it with an egg, and as it boils remove\r
#Text=the scum.
#Text=Keep the peaches in cold water all the time the syrup is\r
#Text=boiling, as water keeps the fruit in good color, while leaving it\r
#Text=exposed darkens it.
#Text=When the syrup has boiled clear, put in the\r
#Text=peaches; let them boil gently for half an hour, then take them out on\r
#Text=a dish for two hours; put them back in the syrup and boil again until\r
#Text=they are clear; they are then done, and you can put them in jars and\r
#Text=pour the syrup over them, and cork and seal up for future use.
#Text=ANOTHER WAY TO PRESERVE PEACHES\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Peel, cut and weigh six pounds of peaches; take six pounds of fine\r
#Text=white sugar, throw the sugar on the peaches until they are well\r
#Text=covered, and let them stay all night.
#Text=Early in the morning add three\r
#Text=pints of water, and boil all together for one hour.
#Text=Skim carefully,\r
#Text=and then take the peaches out on a large dish, still keeping the\r
#Text=syrup gently boiling, and skimming it as it boils.
#Text=Lay the peaches in\r
#Text=the sun on dishes for at least two hours, to harden.
#Text=Taking the fruit\r
#Text=out of the syrup a few times improves it, giving it firmness and\r
#Text=transparency.
#Text=Now replace the peaches in the syrup, and boil gently\r
#Text=until they are clear.
#Text=Cut peaches are much more easily kept than\r
#Text=peaches preserved whole, but they are not so highly flavored.
#Text=Cut\r
#Text=fruit does not require so much boiling as whole fruit; this should be\r
#Text=remembered in preserving.
#Text=PRESERVED CITRON\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Pare off the green skin and all the soft part of the rind, then cut\r
#Text=the firm part in strips, or any shape you fancy.
#Text=Allow a pound and a\r
#Text=quarter of sugar to each pound of rind; line your porcelain kettle\r
#Text=with grapevine leaves and fill with the rind, scattering a little\r
#Text=pulverized alum over each layer.
#Text=Cover with vine-leaves three thick,\r
#Text=pour on water enough to reach and wet these and cover with a close\r
#Text=lid.
#Text=Let them heat together for three hours, but the water must not\r
#Text=actually boil.
#Text=Take out the rind, which will be well greened by this\r
#Text=process, and throw at once into very cold water.
#Text=Let it soak for four\r
#Text=hours, changing the water for fresh every hour.
#Text=Then make a syrup,\r
#Text=allowing two cups of water to every pound and a quarter of syrup.
#Text=Boil and skim until no more scum comes up; put in the rind and simmer\r
#Text=gently nearly an hour.
#Text=Take it out and spread on dishes in the sun\r
#Text=until firm and almost cool.
#Text=Simmer in the syrup for half an hour;\r
#Text=spread out again, and when firm put into a large bowl and pour over\r
#Text=it the scalding syrup.
#Text=Next day put the syrup again over the fire,\r
#Text=add the juice of a lemon and a tiny bit of ginger-root for every\r
#Text=pound of rind.
#Text=Boil down until thick, pack the rind in jars and pour\r
#Text=over it the syrup.
#Text=Tie up when cool.
#Text=TO PRESERVE PEARS\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Take small rich pears, and boil them gently in water until they will\r
#Text=yield to the pressure of the finger.
#Text=They must not be soft, or they\r
#Text=will not preserve well.
#Text=Take them out when a little boiled; let them\r
#Text=cool, and pare them neatly, leaving a little of the stem on, as well\r
#Text=as the blossom end.
#Text=Make a syrup of a pound of sugar to a pound of\r
#Text=fruit, and when it is boiling hot, pour it on the pears; next day\r
#Text=boil them in the syrup till clear, and bottle them for use.
#Text=PINEAPPLE PRESERVES\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Take fine pineapples, cut off all the rough parts, and each apple\r
#Text=in quarters, shaping each piece alike.
#Text=Boil the pineapples in just\r
#Text=enough water to cover them, and put to this water all the cuttings,\r
#Text=so as to make the syrup as rich in flavor as possible.
#Text=When the\r
#Text=pieces are tender, take them out, weigh them, and make a syrup of a\r
#Text=pound of sugar to each pound of fruit, allowing a cup of the water\r
#Text=the pineapples were boiled in, to each pound of fruit.
#Text=Strain the\r
#Text=water over the sugar, mix it, and let it boil fifteen minutes, by\r
#Text=itself; skim it, and put in the pineapples, letting them boil until\r
#Text=they are clear and perfectly tender.
#Text=Pears done in this way make\r
#Text=a delicious preserve.
#Text=The usual way of putting them in the syrup\r
#Text=without previous boiling, makes them little better than sweetened\r
#Text=leather, as it makes them tough and stringy.
#Text=TO PRESERVE CRAB APPLES, GREEN\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Wash the apples and boil them in a very little water, cover them with\r
#Text=vine leaves, while on the fire simmering, and they will then be very\r
#Text=yellow.
#Text=Take them out and spread them on a large dish to cool.
#Text=Pare\r
#Text=and core them, put them back in the kettle, with fresh leaves to\r
#Text=cover them.
#Text=Hang them over the fire, or on the stove in a preserving\r
#Text=kettle until they are green; then take them out of the pot, let them\r
#Text=cool, weigh them, and allow a pound of sugar to a pound of fruit.
#Text=Put\r
#Text=only water sufficient to dissolve the sugar, as the fruit, having\r
#Text=been already boiled, will require very little water--a small cupful\r
#Text=to each pound being quite enough.
#Text=Boil this syrup, skim it, and put\r
#Text=in your green apples, and boil them until they are clear and tender.
#Text=Put the apples in jars, turn the juice on to them, and when cold tie\r
#Text=them up, or rather seal them in this Southern climate.
#Text=FIG PRESERVES\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Boil the sugar and water syrup as directed in previous recipe.
#Text=Let\r
#Text=the figs be firm, not dead ripe or they will boil to a mass.
#Text=They\r
#Text=should be laid in alum the day before they are to be preserved, then\r
#Text=taken out, washed, and put into the boiling syrup.
#Text=Boil for three\r
#Text=hours, or until transparent; then bottle as usual and seal up with\r
#Text=wax.
#Text=MYRTLE ORANGE PRESERVE, OR HOME-MADE LIMES\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Pluck the oranges before they turn yellow; they should be a rich dark\r
#Text=green; cut a hole in the stem end and take out all the white pulp\r
#Text=and seeds; scrape them carefully, grate the rind so as to break the\r
#Text=oil cells, and allow the strong oil to escape.
#Text=Wash them and throw\r
#Text=them into strong salt and water; let them stay in it for three days,\r
#Text=then soak them in fresh water three days.
#Text=When you wish to preserve\r
#Text=them you must boil them in clear water, slowly, in a brass kettle;\r
#Text=cover them with a few orange leaves while boiling, which will green\r
#Text=them, and boil until they are tender, then set them up to cool.
#Text=Weigh\r
#Text=as much sugar as you have oranges, and allow pound for pound; boil\r
#Text=the syrup clear and then put in the oranges; boil gently for half an\r
#Text=hour, or until green and yellow.
#Text=Use only a silver spoon in making\r
#Text=this preserve.
#Text=TO MAKE WATERMELON PRESERVES\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Take the firm outside rind of the watermelon; scrape off the green\r
#Text=and cut out the soft inside; cut the rind into any shapes you choose,\r
#Text=stars, crescents, diamonds, etc.
#Text=After they have been boiled in\r
#Text=alum and leaves to green and harden, weigh them and make a syrup of\r
#Text=a pound of sugar to a pound of fruit, with a cup of water to each\r
#Text=pound.
#Text=Boil the syrup clear, and put in the cut rinds, and boil them\r
#Text=until transparent.
#Text=Flavor with ginger for green color, and lemons for\r
#Text=the yellow.
#Text=If the rind is wanted yellow you must boil it with fresh\r
#Text=lemon skins and a little saffron before preserving it.
#Text=DELICATE PREPARATIONS FOR THE SICK AND CONVALESCENT\r
#Text=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=                            BARLEY WATER\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Take four large tablespoonfuls of picked and washed pearl barley,\r
#Text=and put it into a porcelain-lined kettle with two quarts of boiling\r
#Text=water; let it boil slowly until the water is reduced one half, then\r
#Text=strain it and season with salt, lemon, or sugar as may be agreeable\r
#Text=to the sick.
#Text=TOAST WATER\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Cut two or three slices from a loaf of wheat bread, toast them very\r
#Text=brown; while hot, put them in a small pitcher, and pour over them a\r
#Text=pint and a half of water.
#Text=Sugar may be added if liked, but when the\r
#Text=stomach is affected it is better without it.
#Text=TO MAKE WATER GRUEL OF CORN MEAL OR OAT MEAL\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Put a quart of water on to boil in a stew-pan.
#Text=Take a tablespoonful\r
#Text=of sweet corn meal, or oatmeal, make it into a batter with milk and\r
#Text=salt, stir it in the boiling water and let it boil gently for half an\r
#Text=hour.
#Text=When served it may be sweetened and nutmeg grated over it.
#Text=If\r
#Text=wanted for a strengthening nourishment, a bit of butter and a glass\r
#Text=of wine or brandy may be added.
#Text=This is generally given after a dose\r
#Text=of castor oil, or an emetic.
#Text=Use very little salt.
#Text=BEEF TEA FOR INVALIDS\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Cut tender lean beef into small pieces, free it from fat and strings,\r
#Text=fill a junk bottle with it, cork it tight and put it in a kettle of\r
#Text=boiling water; let it boil three hours.
#Text=In that way you obtain the\r
#Text=juices of the meat undiluted.
#Text=This is especially nourishing and good\r
#Text=when the stomach can bear but little liquid.
#Text=MILK PUNCH AS A RESTORATIVE\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Take a large tumbler (it should hold a pint), half fill it with\r
#Text=chopped ice, add to it a large tablespoonful of white sugar, beat it\r
#Text=a little with the ice, then pour on it a wineglass of gin, rum or\r
#Text=brandy, and fill up with fresh milk.
#Text=It is generally very acceptable\r
#Text=to an invalid who refuses other stimulants.
#Text=APPLE TEA, OR WATER, FOR INVALIDS\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Cut some ripe apples into thin pieces, add the peel of a fresh lemon;\r
#Text=pour boiling water over them and let it stand till cold, then sweeten\r
#Text=with loaf sugar.
#Text=This is a grateful and cooling drink.
#Text=BAKED APPLES\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Bake them in a tin roaster, as iron discolors them; pour molasses\r
#Text=over them and bake until soft.
#Text=This is good for opening the bowels of\r
#Text=patients who are a little constipated.
#Text=ARROW-ROOT BLANC MANGE FOR THE SICK\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Put a pint of new milk to boil; make a smooth batter with an ounce\r
#Text=of Bermuda arrow-root and cold milk; add a little salt, and when the\r
#Text=milk is boiling stir in the batter; let the fire be gentle or it will\r
#Text=scorch; sweeten this with fine white sugar, and let it boil a few\r
#Text=minutes; flavor with lemon, or orange water, or if lemon is objected\r
#Text=to, boil a vanilla bean in the milk before the arrow-root is put in.
#Text=Take it off the fire, pour it in a mould and set it on ice; serve\r
#Text=jelly or jam with the blanc mange, or eat it with cream if it agrees\r
#Text=with the invalid.
#Text=ARROW-ROOT BLANC MANGE\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Mix in a little cold water, two tablespoonfuls of arrow-root; sweeten\r
#Text=a pint of milk with white sugar and put the arrow-root in the milk.
#Text=Let it boil a few minutes, stirring it constantly; take it off, and\r
#Text=if desired, you can let it cool and mould it in a bowl or jelly\r
#Text=form; or it is nice to be eaten warm.
#Text=Colored jelly over it is an\r
#Text=improvement when moulded.
#Text=ARROW-ROOT GRUEL\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Mix a tablespoonful of arrow-root, or for an infant, half as much;\r
#Text=when mixed with cold water, stir in it half a pint of boiling water.
#Text=Season with salt, sugar or nutmeg.
#Text=MILK PORRIDGE\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Make a quart of milk boiling hot; make a tablespoonful of flour\r
#Text=into a batter with cold milk, add a little salt and stir it in the\r
#Text=boiling milk, stirring it constantly for five minutes while it boils;\r
#Text=flavor with anything agreeable.
#Text=Sweetened with loaf sugar, and nutmeg\r
#Text=grated plentifully over it, it will make a most excellent remedy for\r
#Text=looseness or dysentery.
#Text=TAPIOCA MILK\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Wash and soak a large tablespoonful of tapioca, put it to a quart of\r
#Text=sweet milk, add a little salt, cover it, and set it over a gentle\r
#Text=fire for an hour.
#Text=Take it up, add sugar and nutmeg, or cinnamon to\r
#Text=taste.
#Text=TAPIOCA PUDDING\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Put a coffee-cup of tapioca (soak it well first) into a pint and a\r
#Text=half of milk, set it where it will get hot slowly, take it off when\r
#Text=it boils, and when cool add four well-beaten eggs; flavor with lemon\r
#Text=and peach, sweeten it to taste, and bake for an hour in a hot oven.
#Text=If this is wanted for one person, take half the quantity of tapioca\r
#Text=and milk.
#Text=WHITE WINE SYLLABUB\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Season a pint of milk with sugar and wine, but not enough wine to\r
#Text=curdle the milk.
#Text=Fill your glasses nearly full, and crown them with\r
#Text=sweetened whipped cream.
#Text=Season the cream with extract of lemon.
#Text=SYLLABUB\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Take the juice of a large lemon, and the yellow rind pared thin; one\r
#Text=glass of brandy, two glasses of white wine, and a quarter of a pound\r
#Text=of powdered sugar.
#Text=Put these ingredients into a pan, and let them\r
#Text=remain one night; the next day add a pint of thick cream, and the\r
#Text=whites of two eggs beaten together; beat them all together to a fine\r
#Text=froth, and serve in jelly glasses.
#Text=WINE SANGAREE OF PORT OR MADEIRA\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Take half a glass of water, sweeten it with a tablespoonful of white\r
#Text=powdered sugar, and stir well until dissolved; add a gill of Madeira\r
#Text=or Port, some nutmeg grated and pounded ice.
#Text=Serve with lady-cake or\r
#Text=pound-cake, cut small.
#Text=STEWED PRUNES FOR SICKNESS\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Wash the prunes, put them in a stew pan, cover them with water, and\r
#Text=to each pound of prunes put a cupful of clear brown sugar.
#Text=Cover the\r
#Text=stew-pan and let them boil slowly, until the syrup is thick and rich.
#Text=WINE JELLY FOR THE SICK\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Take one pint of Madeira wine, one pint of water, and one ounce of\r
#Text=isinglass dissolved in a teacupful of water.
#Text=Let the wine and water\r
#Text=be boiling hot, then stir into it the dissolved isinglass, and\r
#Text=sugar to taste; make it quite sweet; let it come to a boil, try it\r
#Text=by taking a little in a saucer, and if not a good jelly when cold,\r
#Text=boil it until it is so; if lemon is allowed, use the juice of two to\r
#Text=flavor this jelly.
#Text=JAUNE MANGE\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Break up and boil an ounce of isinglass in rather more than half a\r
#Text=pint of water until it is melted; strain it; then add the juice of\r
#Text=two large oranges, a gill of white wine, and the yolks of four eggs\r
#Text=beaten and strained; sweeten to taste, and stir it over a gentle\r
#Text=fire till it boils up; dip a mould into cold water and pour the\r
#Text=preparation into it.
#Text=CARRIGEEN MOSS FOR INVALIDS\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Wash and pick a tablespoonful of Irish moss and put it into a tin\r
#Text=cup; pour on it half a pint of boiling water, and set it on the coals\r
#Text=for a short time; when it is all dissolved add sugar and nutmeg to\r
#Text=taste.
#Text=This may be made with milk, to resemble custard, and is very\r
#Text=nourishing.
#Text=Delicate infants may be fed on it when they will take no\r
#Text=other nourishment.
#Text=TARTARIC ACID AS A SUBSTITUTE FOR LEMONS\r
#Text=\r
#Text=If lemons cannot be obtained to make either a lemonade or jellies\r
#Text=for the sick, tartaric acid is a good substitute, and if used in\r
#Text=conjunction with the extract of lemon, is a very agreeable one.
#Text=LEMON JELLY WITHOUT LEMONS\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Take a box of Cox’s gelatine, pour over it one quart of boiling\r
#Text=water, and stir until it is dissolved.
#Text=Add a teaspoonful of tartaric\r
#Text=acid, and four cups of sugar; let it dissolve and bring it to a boil;\r
#Text=while boiling, stir in the beaten whites of three eggs; let this\r
#Text=boil up once again and take it off the fire; when nearly cool, add\r
#Text=to it a tablespoonful of good extract of lemon.
#Text=Strain the mixture\r
#Text=into moulds or cups, and set it in a cool place, or on ice, to become\r
#Text=firm.
#Text=It must be cool, or it will not jelly.
#Text=ORANGE SHERBET\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Squeeze the juice from a dozen oranges; pour boiling water on the\r
#Text=peel, and cover it closely.
#Text=Boil water and sugar (a pint to a pound)\r
#Text=to a syrup; skim it clear; when all are cold, mix the syrup, juice\r
#Text=and peel with as much water as may be necessary to make a rich\r
#Text=orangeade; strain it, and set the vessel containing it on ice.
#Text=Or it\r
#Text=may be made the same as lemonade, using one lemon with half a dozen\r
#Text=oranges.
#Text=STRAWBERRY SHERBET\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Take fifteen ounces of picked strawberries, crush them in a mortar,\r
#Text=then add to them a quart of water; pour this into a basin, with\r
#Text=a sliced lemon, and a teaspoonful of orange-flower water; let\r
#Text=it remain for two or three hours.
#Text=Put eighteen ounces of sugar\r
#Text=into another basin, cover it with a cloth, through which pour the\r
#Text=strawberry juice; after as much has run through as will, gather\r
#Text=up the cloth, and squeeze out as much juice as possible from it;\r
#Text=when the sugar is all dissolved, strain it again.
#Text=Set the vessel\r
#Text=containing it on ice, until ready to serve.
#Text=ALMOND CUSTARD\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Blanch and beat four ounces of almonds fine, with a spoonful of\r
#Text=water; beat a pint of cream with two spoonfuls of rose water, add\r
#Text=them to the yolks of four eggs and as much sugar as will make it\r
#Text=pretty sweet; stir it over a slow fire till it is of a proper\r
#Text=thickness, but do not boil.
#Text=Pour it into custard glasses.
#Text=SPONGE CAKE PUDDING\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Stale sponge or other plain cake may be made into a nice pudding by\r
#Text=crumbling it into a little more than a pint of milk and two or three\r
#Text=beaten eggs, and baking it.
#Text=Sauce--sugar and butter beaten together.
#Text=GERMAN LADIES’ FINGERS\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Beat one hour the yolks of five eggs with half a pound of sugar; add\r
#Text=half a pound of blanched almonds pounded fine, the yellow part of one\r
#Text=lemon grated.
#Text=Mix well; add half a pound of flour very gradually.
#Text=Roll out the paste, and cut it into strips the length and size of the\r
#Text=forefinger; beat lightly the whites of two eggs, and wet the fingers.
#Text=DIMPLES\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Beat the whites of three eggs very stiff, add gradually three\r
#Text=quarters of a pound of sugar, and beat till it is well mixed.
#Text=Blanch\r
#Text=almonds, and cut them into pieces--as small as peas, and stir them\r
#Text=into the egg and sugar--three quarters of a pound of almonds for\r
#Text=three eggs.
#Text=Drop the mixture in spots as large as a half penny on\r
#Text=white paper upon a tin, and bake in a cool oven.
#Text=DELICATE RUSKS FOR CONVALESCENTS\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Half a pint of new milk, and one cup of hop yeast; add flour to make\r
#Text=a batter, and set the sponge at night.
#Text=In the morning add half a pint\r
#Text=of milk, one cup of sugar, one of butter, one egg, one nutmeg, and\r
#Text=flour to make it sufficiently stiff.
#Text=Let it rise, then roll it, and\r
#Text=cut it out; let it rise again, and then bake.
#Text=CHOCOLATE CARAMELS\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Half a pound of chocolate, three pounds of dark brown sugar,\r
#Text=one-eighth pound of butter, a small teacup of milk; season with\r
#Text=vanilla, or grated lemon or orange-peel.
#Text=Boil it very quickly over a\r
#Text=hot fire, stirring constantly.
#Text=When it becomes hard on being dropped\r
#Text=into water, take it off the fire and stir for a few moments before\r
#Text=pouring into buttered dishes.
#Text=Before it is quite cool, cut into\r
#Text=little squares.
#Text=Those who like the caramel very hard need not stir\r
#Text=it, as this makes it “sugary.”
#Text=The grated peel should not be put in\r
#Text=till the caramel is taken from the fire.
